title: practical english usage - Part 344
content:
Could you stand over there, please? (polite request) Please stand over there, (more polite order) Stand over there, (order) Note that please does not change an order into a request. Compare: 'Wouldyou like some help? 'Yes, please.' Could I have some more rice, please?

We   use please to make requests more polite.

556  please       and   thank       you I'll never forget seeing Olivier play Othello.

in a play or film.

Play can be used with the same meaning as act before the name of a character Have you ever played rugby football?

My daughter is acting in her school play this year.

People act in plays or films, and play games or musical instruments.

Children learn a great deal through play.

The  uncountable noun play can be used to mean 'playing' in general.

Chess is a very slow game, (     . ..a very slow play.) A game is an activity like, for example, chess, football or bridge.

'Julius Caesar' is one of Shakespeare's early plays.

A  play is a piece of dramatic literature for the theatre, radio or television.

555  play     and   game For similar structures with way, time and reason, ►    237.7.

That boy's going to go places, believe me.

Go places (informal) means 'become very successful in life'.

clothes. (\Di  ...                             wash mydolhes.) I'm looking for a place (where) I can wash my clothes. / a place to wash my We   do not use a place where before an infinitive.

(= .. . the place (that) we had lunch at?   the place where we had lunch?) You remember the place we had lunch?

There's no place to sit down.

... a place in which to live.) or I'm looking for a place to live. (More formal:. .. a place to live in relative clause, with no preposition or relative word.

In an informal style, place can often be followed directly by an infinitive or 554  place:     a  place      to   live,    etc Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  557  point of view Thank you for giving us your point of view / opinion.

Point of view can mean the same as opinion.

557  point      of   view For more about the language of common social situations, ►    329.

‘ Thanks so much  for looking after the children.' ‘ That's all right. Any time.' ‘ Here's your coat.' ‘ Thanks.' (no answer) ‘ Could you pass the salt? ‘ Hereyou are.' ‘ Thanks.' (no answer) Compare: (quite) all right or That's OK  (informal). Some people say No problem (informal).

reply is necessary, we  can say Not at all (rather formal), You're welcome, That's especially, do not usually answer when they are thanked for small things. If a In English, there is not an automatic answer to Thank you; British people, 5 replies     to  thanks ‘ Have you got enough potatoes? ‘ Yes, thanks.' Yes, thanks is most often used to confirm that things are all right.

‘ Another cake? ‘ No, thanks. I've eaten too many already.' No, thank you / No, thanks.

To  make it clear that one wishes to refuse something, it is normal to say ‘ Wouldyou like some potatoes? ‘ Thank you.' ‘ How many?

We   often use Thank you / Thanks like Yes, please, to accept offers.

4 accepting       and   refusing Some British people say Cheers (informal) to mean Thanks.

(  i  Thank-yim for ytntr cmning.) ‘ Thank you for coming.' ‘ Not at all. Thank you for having me.' unnecessary and are not used.

Thank you for / Thanks for can be followed by an -ing form. Possessives are but notusually Thank you very much  indeed, (                      Thanh you indeed.) Indeed (►  499) can be used to strengthen very much.  This is rather formal.

Thank goodness it's Friday, (     Thanks goodness-...) but not Thanks very much.         Thanks a lot. (          Thank-you-a-lot.) not Thank you. (     Thanks you/) Thank you very much.

Thanks is more informal than thank you. Common expressions: 3 thank       you     and   thanks not ‘ Thanks a lot.' ‘ That's OK  . (     ... Please/) Please is not used as an answer to Thank you (see below).

‘ Have you got a pen I could use? ‘ Yes, here you are.' (     ... Please.) We   do not use please when we  give things to people.
